Hey everybody, Jordan Belford here and I want to congratulate you on purchasing the Straight Line Marketing Home Study Course. By implementing these strategies that you'll learn in this program, you'll be leagues ahead of 99% of the marketers and entrepreneurs that are active today. I personally invested over two years developing and it represents literally the best of the best of every tactic and strategy that I've perfected over the years that's related to marketing. Now before we go into the details, I first want you to know exactly why I created Straight Line Marketing and how I went about designing it. First you know me as the Wolf of Wall Street and of course I've been described as the world's number one sales trainer time and time again. Now obviously I'm a firm believer in the power of sales and persuasion. In fact, it's the very reason that I developed my proprietary Straight Line system, which is the same system I used to train literally thousands of salespeople and turn them into millionaires. You see, your ability to influence and persuade is the number one distinction between those who achieve master's success and those who basically fail miserably. But here's the thing, I've spent the last eight, nine years traveling all over the world training countless salespeople, marketers, entrepreneurs and literally transforming their lives and their businesses in the process. I've been inundated with feedback from students telling me that the Straight Line system has allowed them to convert a staggering percentage of their leads into new customers. However, they all seem to have the same problem. How do I get more leads? Well, the answer obviously is kick-ass marketing and not just any marketing. You see, the laws of persuasion are just that. Their laws, their universal, the principles of persuasion are the same whether they're face-to-face over the phone, on the internet and that's the problem with a lot of the marketing training that's out there today. They fail to give the students the persuasion system that they need to build their campaigns around. You see, Straight Line marketing has been designed to not only teach you the technical side of developing a killer digital strategy, but to also give you the psychology that you need to build campaigns that are so compelling and so effective that you'll be able to literally blow your competitors out of the water. Now, there's actually an interesting story to how this product came to be. During my speaking tours around the world, I lecture thousands and thousands of marketers every year in the psychology of persuasion, which had to apply the principles of the Straight Line system to their existing marketing efforts and show them the huge impact it has on their results. Now, it was like, I think three or four years ago, you know, Australia, and I met a team of marketing experts that were running a successful digital marketing agency and I taught them Straight Line persuasion and they were blown away. Now, I'm actually going to pass things back on to Jordan, where again, he's going to go through the fundamentals of the straight line persuasion system. By Spakalarp, if you haven't been exposed to the SLP system before, it is an awesomely powerful persuasive technology. And it really is going to form the backbone. It is the engine that's going to drive this SLM system. It is what has produced the most significant and obvious difference to our business and to all of our marketing efforts. It really will change the way you approach a business and how you actually try to persuade people to take a particular action, which is what online marketing is all about. And it will change the way you do that forever. Please listen very carefully. This is your fundamentals. This is your fundamentals. This is going to form the foundation of everything that we cover from here onward. So I'll pass things back on to Jordan, where he'll explain the nuances and the details of the straight line persuasion system. Before you have a reasonable shot of closing someone, and let me tell you exactly what they are. Number one, whatever is your selling, whatever product it is, and that product could be a tangible product, like a car, it could be an informational product, it could be simply yourself, right, an idea that you have. But whatever that product is, the first thing that you have to establish in a situation of influence, you're trying to close somebody, is they have to believe that your product is, I like to say, the best thing since sliced bread. In other words, it's not okay if you, let's say you're selling a car, for instance, and it's just, you'll use something very, very tangible. And let's say someone's going to buy a Volkswagen, right, just any car under the sun, right, a Volkswagen. So if someone goes to the dealership to buy a Volkswagen, and they walk in and they see the salesperson, and the salesperson explains the things about the car that matter, all the relevant features of the car, right, and all the benefits even more important than the features. And after the salesperson is done, he takes the person for a test ride, and the person, the salesperson, what do you think, you know, how do you like the car? And the person says, eh, it's okay. But yeah, it's pretty good, which is a very typical response you might get. Oh yeah, it's a nice car. That's not the level of certainty that's necessary for you to actually close the deal. Someone's got to say, wow, yeah, I love, I love this car, it's phenomenal. When you get someone to that level of certainty about your product, about your idea, whatever it is that you're selling, right, could be information. And someone says, wow, no, that's amazing, you have a shot of influencing them. That's the first thing. The second thing that has to occur is that the person who you're trying to influence has to connect with you. So you have to essentially sell yourself. So as important as it is that they love your product, it's equally important that they connect with you on both the conscious and the unconscious level as the influencer. So what I'm getting at here, watch. So it's okay. Yeah, the product is on a scale of one to ten, you've got to get into a ten on your product. Next, you've got to get into a ten about yourself. You know, who are you? As a salesperson, as an influencer, what do you represent? What are your standards? Are you a person with a lot of integrity and ethics? Are you a person that prides themselves on customer service? Are you a person that plans on being the top producer in your firm and you're not going to get there by being wrong? Are you a person that works mostly on referrals? Are you a person that works on one-shot deals? All these different, those are called language patterns. I'll just give you some, a couple of language patterns, we'll get to that later. But things that you say about yourself, the actual words themselves, can either create the image in someone's heart and in their mind that, wow, this is a person that I can trust. This is a person that I can do business with and feel comfortable. They're not going to essentially rip my eyeballs out for a commission. So it's very important that if you want to influence and close the deal, you have to line up A, the product to attend, B, yourself to attend, of certainty that you're the great person worth doing business with. And lastly, the third 10 is your actual company. And this means that the actual company that stands behind the product is honest, is ethical, they have integrity, they have a blue chip reputation. So look at this way, let's say someone, let's look back at a recent history when Toyota was having all those problems. Toyota is a great company, we all know that, right? They made their mistakes, every company makes mistakes. But there was this little blip when it was all over the press that the cars were accelerating by themselves and people died and Toyota was wearing that black dark hat and people were saying, wow, it's not a company that we can trust, which is really sad because I'm sure that Toyota is a company that can be trusted, they just had a bad time of it, right? But during that blip, that moment of time, what happened to Toyota's sales? They went plummeting through the floor because no matter how much you might love a Toyota, the car itself, no matter how great the salesman, how great the customer service is, if you think, whoa, whoa, whoa, this company does not stand behind their product, they're being a bit shoddy with workmanship, they're not going to be there, this whole aura that's around the Toyota for that brief moment in time, it plunged their sales by like 60%. You get that? So what happened there is, yeah, you can love the product, you can love the salesperson, but you don't trust the company, the equation doesn't work. So again, what we're looking to do here is an influencer who is looking to line up these three things in a single moment in time. So they're at a 10, 10, 10, at which point we then ask for the order or ask to close the deal, depending on what it is that you're selling, whether it's, again, it's information or it could be even a negotiation. When you get a person to see things your way, what you're trying to get the product in a negotiation could be your point of view, right? And it would be that you being an honest negotiator, you're not going to hoodwink them and pull the wall over their eyes, and also that the company is going to live up to its side of the bargain, right? So even in a negotiation, it's again about developing these single three things in a moment of time and then closing the deal. Now how we actually do that, you know, the question is, okay, I got that, right? Makes perfect sense. The question is, how do you do that? How do you do that in a way that's elegant? How do you do that in a way that's simple so it empowers you, meaning that you don't say, God, you know, I mean, yeah, how could I get someone, you know, in one single moment in time? It just seems like a very, very difficult task. And I was aware of that in 1989 when I first developed the straight line system and I owned the brokerage firm called Stratton Oakmont, which was nothing back then, 10 to 12 stockbrokers, and I was trying to train them to speak to the wealthiest 1% of Americans. And it was a difficult task because we're dealing with sophisticated businessmen and I had kids in my boardroom, they were, you know, 18, 19 years old. The oldest was probably 23 or 24 years old. There wasn't a lot of sales experience in the room. There weren't these sort of slick talking, you know, closer types, right? So I was dealing with sort of this, you know, raw clay and I had to mold it and come up with a system that was a simple meaning it has to be very, very easy to use. Second, it has to be duplicatable. In other words, that after you teach to someone, they can a, be teach to someone else and then see you can have a sales manager that can take the system and spread it out as a firm grows and grows and grows. See, sometimes you'll notice that someone's a great sales person. They're a great closer and you walk into an office and the office is to get 20 salespeople and I get this all the time because I do corporate trainings and there's 20 salespeople and 19 of them stink and can't close anything and one guy's hitting it out of the park. So you'd say, well, why don't the other 19 people model the one guy who's hitting it out of the park? The problem is the way that guy's hitting out of the park is typically not modelable. I don't know if that's a word modelable, but you can't model what they're doing because they're doing something that's sort of like they're circling around and they're doing, they're actually running a straight line pattern. I'll explain to you what that is in a minute, but they're doing it in a way that's so complicated and difficult that other people can't duplicate it. So the true beauty of the straight line system is that it's so simple and it's so ABC step by step, paint by numbers, move by move that it allows anybody, even if you have zero sales ability, zero experience in the art of persuasion or influence. It allows any person to become competent. Look at it this way. It takes someone who is the worst closer in the world and makes them basically competent. It takes someone who's a competent closer and makes them great. It takes a great person and makes them unbelievable and it takes that rare unbelievable, natural closer and takes them off the charts to the stratosphere. So that's what the system really does. It makes you the best closer you could possibly be and a close that's congruent with exactly the type of person that you are. So if you are the hard charging type 8 personality, you can adopt that to the straight line system. If you're more of the laid backs, slower talking and you're more conversational and consultative versus closing, straight line is elegant for that just as well. It fits into all influencing situations and what it does more than anything, it's an overall structure, it's a discipline and a goal oriented system of communication. Now I'm going to draw it out for you right here. This is the second key piece of content I want you to focus in on. So grab your pen again. Now I'm going to draw this straight line. You're going to see me do this a couple of times during this webinar. So the way I do it is here, I'm not the greatest artist in the world, this is your straight line and what this represents essentially it's the perfect sale, the perfect situation of influence, the perfect close and what I mean by that is this. Let's just say that if you're in sales you've probably had a couple of these, not that many throughout your life where it's basically a lay down. Everything that you say the person is like oh yeah, no, it sounds great, terrific and you tell the person a bit about your product like oh my god that's just what I needed. You ask the person if it makes sense and they say oh my god it makes perfect sense to me. You tell them a bit about the price like wow that's really really cheap, how do I get signed up. You say well give me your credit card and they're like okay great. He's like right you get the picture it's this perfect sale. So what that represents is this. Everything that you say, watch this okay, it's staying perfectly down the line. Smooth and elegant, no wasted time and again we know from Euclid right the famous geometry guy from you know I don't know how many thousand years ago right. Euclid came up with the statement that the shortest distance between two points is a straight line. So what that sale, that straight line sale represents is the perfect most elegant sale where it's basically a lay down. Where everything you say, every question that you ask, every trial close that you put out there comes back with the perfect response with a person's enthusiastic, with a person's right on target and you just know that the person's going to go right elegantly into the close. Now then we all know of course that that's very far if you between those people and they don't really happen that much in the real world. Typically if you're a goal just imagine in your mind your goal before you even start a conversation. Remember the book The Art of War Sun Tzu, some of you probably read this book, famous book that I read many many years ago and one of the most famous lines of Sun Tzu is every battle is won before it's ever fought. Now I'll repeat that again, every battle is won before it's ever fought. On some level that's essentially what you're doing with the straight line. What you're doing is you're anticipating before you ever open up your mouth for syllable one, everything that could possibly go wrong, every permutation, every variation of the encounter and you're ready for it. Now just so you know, I'm going to jump ahead just for a split second here with another piece of content is that there are a couple of other issues here that are very very important in terms of influence. Now the three tens is on one side of the equation, these are essentially your positives, you know your positive about you, positive about your product, right? And positive about your company. You also have these other things that have to fall into place as well and one of them is someone's pain threshold. That is how much pain are they feeling in the moment right now about not having your product, your good or your service or having you as a coach, whatever it is that you're trying to sell, okay, or influence someone with, right? So if someone's not feeling that much pain about their current situation, well they're a bit harder to influence, right? You know the house ain't on fire, they're not feeling you know that you know they're really losing out in anything so sometimes what you have to do well in the straight line is actually raise someone's pain threshold, actually let them feel some of the pain that's going to happen if they don't take action and I caution you, I mean in actually I have a home study course which I'll talk about in a little while of how you can actually master this technique right at home, okay, in a very elegant way and in a fun way too, but what happens is to raise someone's pain threshold, you've got to be careful because you can very quickly come off like an asshole. If you want to make someone feel pain just for pain's sake that's not a very good thing to do but to actually allow someone to realize what's at stake and essentially if they don't take action, meaning to keep living their life without your product, your good, your service, your coaching program, whatever it is, without you in their life if they can actually now set the rules, you know wow my life is not working the way it is right now, not getting what I want, I don't feel inspired, I don't feel empowered and I'm sorry not feeling that I'm getting everything I should out of life, when you get someone to that point it becomes easier to close the deal. So that's one of the other things we're going to talk about after the fact, another piece of content. The next thing is called your action threshold. I'm a terrible writer of course, as I said before. So your action threshold, that is the level of certainty that you need to get to or if it's somebody else that someone else needs to get to before they say yes, I'm going to buy. In other words, some people like me, I'm a very, very easy target for sales people, I just buy shit, okay, I have beliefs in sign, these are by the way your action threshold is made up of beliefs, to some of what you believe about buying, what you believe about sales people, what you believe about taking action, about risk, people with very, very high action thresholds, people that don't buy easily, you have to get them up to a very, very high level of certainty on the three tenths. Conversely, people like me with a low action threshold, well the truth is I don't really have to be at a ten, ten, ten, right? I could be reasonably certain about the product, I could say the salesman's decent person, not the best, they're decent, and the company, they're okay, they're not, you know, they're not scammers, but they're not, you know, blue chip all the way, right? But if someone like me who's reasonably certain with a low action threshold, I'll buy. People like my dad who's, you know, has a high action threshold, he doesn't like to buy from anybody, right, unless he is absolutely certain of the three tenths. So now let's put it all together here before I take you down the straight line here, is that what happens is this, as you're going from the open here to the close here, what's happening is, is you're simultaneously building up the three tenths, you're getting so into a higher level of certainty about your product, about yourself, and about your company, and at the same time, you're actually raising up the pain threshold if it's necessary, and if they're already feeling the pain, and you'll know that, because they'll talk about how screwed up things are for them, they're actually talking about their pain, and you don't really have to raise their pain threshold up that month, but if they're in denial, whether they're like pretending they don't need anything that you have in their life, perfect the way it is, sometimes it will actually take a little work on your part to raise up the pain issue to make them realize what's truly at stake if they don't take action. Okay, so now let me put it all together, I'm going to turn the sheet. Okay, and let me go through visually here what exactly happens on the straight line, and again, I don't have much time here, if we were in person and you were at one of my live events, I would go on for days and days, and I know on the computer there's a sort of limited attention span, I get that, you're at home probably, or at work, and you only have maybe an hour at most, so I'm going to keep it brief and give you enough things, and I think I already gave you hopefully one piece that you can start using at least a little bit right now, even if you don't know the whole system, just a little bit to make a difference, but now let me try to pull it all together for you really, really quickly here, so you can understand exactly what the straight line is, and I think you'll see pretty quickly how you can take this, and it doesn't matter what you're selling or influencing, negotiating, it works for any situation at all. It works of course with someone on the phone looking to close a deal, whether it's stocks, bonds, currency trading, a system selling software, it doesn't matter what you're selling on the phone, if it's in person, okay, it's exactly the same thing, it works beautifully for high ticket items where sometimes the sales could take, or the close could take three months, you have to go through different layers of management to actually get to the ultimate decision maker or multiple decision makers, it really shows you how to navigate through every possible closing scenario that's out there, and I've developed it through trial and error because I've been in every business imaginable and trained just about every sort of sales force that you can imagine, I mean it just goes on and on, so that being said, let me draw the straight line one more time, okay, now remember this is the, okay, this is the open and this is the close, okay, and what you have here is that's your perfect sale, right, and remember just the visual that from the second that you start the encounter, your goals are constantly trying to push the person, when I say push, I don't mean in a negative way, just guide the person down the straight line from the open to the close, and the close, depending on what it is you're selling, it could be simply getting the credit card number, it could be if you're, it could be raising money for charity, I've dealt with charities and shown them how to use this system to effectively raise more money for a cause, right, it could be getting the credit card number, getting commitment from, from it's for a charity, it doesn't matter what it is, it could be for a political situation trying to get people to sign up to your party, it doesn't matter, okay, so this is your perfect sale, you're open, you're closed, and now we have the healthy boundaries above and below, because we know from experience that typically most sales like 99.9% of sales will not follow the direct straight line, so what you have here is this is your healthy boundaries above and below the line, when you're in these boundaries, you're in control of the, in terms of controlling the sale, when you're outside of these boundaries, either above or above or below, you're off, here we'll call this Pluto, and here we'll call this your anus, or your anus, okay, your anus, okay, above and below the line, this is bad, this is bad, in here it's good, so what that really means is this follows, if this represents the perfect presentation, where you're actually taking the person, telling them about your product, your service, right, and you're asking them questions, you know, qualifying them, trying to find out what their pain is, what their need is, what they're looking to do, what their outcome is, so you very often what I find with people who aren't trained in straight line technology is that they go into a sale or an influence situation with a preconceived notion of what this person wants, and they're going off their own map of the world, they're saying, well, I want, you know, I know what would be important to me if I bought this product, and they start trying to put that onto the prospect, when in fact the prospect could have a complete different set of needs and wants that they have, so here they are trying to influence somebody not knowing really what the person wants, not knowing what their pain really is, and that's very hard to do, so we find that out by being very good question askers, we become masters of asking open-ended questions, starting with big picture questions to identify exactly what someone's needs are, to identify exactly what their hurts are, what their pains are, bad experiences they've had, we find out exactly if they could imagine tailoring a product just to suit their needs, what would that be, what would be the best of the best, and what would be the worst of the worst, I'll tell you a story, I once went into a situation where I made a big mistake and I learned just a heart, I went into a company a couple of years ago and I went in there and I was selling my own services, it was a big company, a very fortunate $109 billion company, and I went in there thinking that they want to learn how to close and do this and do that, and in fact it had nothing to do with that at all, and I gave them this whole pitch and I ended up not getting the deal, and I was like wow, I'm surprised because I thought I gave a class A presentation, later on my manager called up and said they absolutely loved you, but what they were looking for was someone to talk about customer service, get it, they weren't interested in a closing presentation, and by the way the straight line is amazing for customer service, I'm telling you this, for customer service and developing long term relationships and how you deal with complaints and how you get more referrals, the straight line is beautiful, but I didn't know what they really wanted because I didn't ask the right questions, I went off my preconceived map of what someone would want, and in this particular case, and very often I'm right, I think I know what people want, but I learned that one day never, never take that for granted, you need to ask the right questions starting with big quick picture questions, and then slowly tailor it down, I would give you a list, I don't have time right now, but in the home study course it breaks this down, add noisiness to the point where you know every question, which ones would apply to your specific industry and what not. So now here's what happens, let's just break this down for a second, when you're on the straight line, it's basically you're talking or asking a question and they're giving you the exact response that you want, it's the perfect sale that goes all the way to the close, when you go off the line, in this boundary there are two things happening, you're developing rapport, and next you're gathering intelligence, those are the two things that are happening, and they happen for the entire conversation, you're always gathering intelligence, you're always building on that rapport from the beginning and getting it stronger and stronger and stronger. Now what happens, and of course there are certain points when you are in the body of your presentation here, where you're not asking as many questions, but what's happening also is remember this, is very often you're going to learn that when you start to talk you're going to see by the responses that they get that you can either shorten your presentation and move further down the line, or you might have to ask what's called loop back, because something is not playing along with you, essentially they're not giving you the enthusiastic yes response, you say does that make sense, they're like eh, well you can't go forward right, if they're not saying they love your product, you need to get into a higher level of certainty, so you're going to learn that, well of course you've got to develop rapport instantly, you've got to gather massive intelligence the whole way through, you're going to learn very easy, especially when you dig into the home study course, it's so easy to do, how to actually navigate someone from the open to the close, building up those three tens, and this is really the section in here, you start really building up those three tens on the product, on yourself and on your company, and at this point here is where you learn how to lower their action threshold, which is the beliefs that they have, raise up any pain that they need to feel to understand what's at risk here, if they don't take action, and of course in the beginning here, while you're doing the bulk of your qualifying here and developing rapport, you need to make sure, and you need to be very, very careful, you need to make sure that you're listening effectively, and what that means is that when you ask someone a question, if you say what's the biggest problem you're facing right now in your current business, and let's just say, well the problem is my competition is undercutting me on price right now, and it's causing me to have to borrow more money and get more aggressive credit lines, and whatever they say, and you say, okay, and then you just move on, and how many people do you employ, now that's a big mistake, and you doctrack, you ask the first question, they give you an answer, and you like move on, and okay, now how many people do you employ, you didn't respond to their answer, in other words, it's these little things that you might say, so they say, well I'm having problems, you're like, mm, really, I get it, mm, I totally see what you're saying, uh-huh, it's that, you're understanding that you're feeling their pain, and a lot of that comes through not the words that you say, but the tonality in which you say it, now we're running out of time here fast, I'm gonna give you one last piece of content before we close, okay, and this is the ability of how you use tonality to close the deal, or how to use tonality, essentially, to become a more effective communicator, there's a model that many of you might have seen before, it's a neuro-linguistic programming model, and what it basically says is that in human communication, only 9%, let me see, there you go, only 9%, oh I see it's double sided, okay, 9% of communication is the actual words, and then you have this other split of the other 91%, it's called 46%, is body language, which is, you know, how you move your body, the management of space and time when you're in front of somebody, and the other 55%, 45% is tonality, and that is how you actually say the words that you say. Two things here, okay, number one, if your prospect does not think that you care, and if your prospect does not think that you're trustworthy, good luck trying to influence them, good luck trying to close the deal. Now here's the way you do this, the way you establish that you care and that you're trustworthy is not so much through the words that you say, more than anything in this department when it comes to words, it's about, excuse my expression here, not saying stupid shit that makes them instantly realize that you don't care, that you're not trustworthy, but that, let's just say you don't say anything stupid the way they truly, on an unconscious, on an emotional level, connect with the fact that you are a person that really is, wow, this person really cares about my needs, they seem really trustworthy, that happens unconsciously, and it happens through the use of effective toneality. So, you know, I'll give you an example, when I was young, my mom would say, Jordan, or Jordan, two different meanings to the same word. Now, on a phone conversation, let's say, because typically, even if you go out into the field and you sell in person, initial communication very often happens over the telephone, right? You have four seconds, that's it, you have four seconds to establish three things with another person, then if you don't establish these three things, write this down please, these three things, if you don't establish these three things in the first four seconds of a conversation, you have a very, very difficult time influencing the person down the road, meaning whether it's in five minutes later or an hour later or three months later, you have four seconds, then you have an additional, let's say, ten seconds, if you scoop the first four to play catch up ball, where you can actually still do a little bit of makeup here, but typically, if you don't get them in the first four seconds, you're done. So, let me, and that's because we do judge books by their covers. So, that instant first impression that you make is absolutely critical. I'll tell you what these three things are. Number one, they need to know right off the bat that you're sharp as a tack. Number two, enthusiastic as hell. Number three, an expert in your field. An expert in your field. Sharp as a tack, when you're on the ball. Two, you're enthusiastic about what you're talking about. And three, that you're an expert in your field. And what this, the reason why this is so important is what this really chunks up to, it's what I said earlier. It's that when people think that about you, they're like, wow, this is a person worth listening to. And the reason we listen is because at the higher level, what we're saying to ourselves is this is a person who can help me achieve my own goals, get them their own outcomes. Now, does that mean that you close everybody? No, of course not. No matter how great your marketing is, no matter how defined the marketing is, meaning if it puts just the right people into the sales funnel, if you're off base, even a little bit with your marketing, of course you'll get people into the funnel that really don't qualify. They shouldn't be there in the first place. Or even if your marketing is picture perfect, you're still going to get people that really aren't buyers. Not everyone is going to close no matter what, but the goal of the straight line system, the technology itself, is to use the core elements of human influence packaged together in a way that's easy to learn, that's low pressure for most people. Most people don't want to use pressure when they sell. Now, you can use pressure with the straight line for sure. It shows you how to use higher levels of pressure, but I caution you that make sure that above all, always put your ethics and integrity first. The system is so powerful and you see when you really learn to use the straight line and to close anyone who's closeable, you can actually close people who shouldn't be closed, who should not buy your product, who aren't qualified to buy your product, who don't really need your product, or you can get people to buy more of it. In the mean time, they want a little bit of your product, they can need a little bit, but you sell them more. Don't do that. Don't use this technique for that outcome. Use it to empower people, use it to get people to take action when they should to get the things they need in life to allow them to live more fulfilling lives, to empower them. Of course, as a result of that, you get exactly what you want. So there you have it. Straight line persuasion 101, whether you're already an SLP pro or you're brand new to the system, what you've just learned is going to be crucial as you work your way through the straight line marketing system. Remember, everything you're about to learn about straight line marketing is built on the principles taught in this module. Now, I want you to go through your notes, review them, go through the exercises in your workbook, and prepare yourself a module too where the real world application begins.
